narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a challenging one—69% likelihood of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students just like you. Picture your academic journey as the opening chapters of your story: your Year 1 grades placed you in the bottom decile at school, setting a shaky foundation that carried the heaviest weight in our model's view, pulling strongly toward the risk of failure. By Year 3, that bottom decile ranking again stood out as the top influence, signaling to the model that even with time, those early struggles persisted and made passing the bar seem unlikely. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 23.5, which ranked second in impact and whispered doubts about handling the intense legal reasoning the bar demands. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.1 added to this narrative of academic hurdles, ranking sixth but still tilting the scales toward difficulty. As an Asian female from a lower-middle income family pursuing full-time studies, these details wove in as supporting threads—your background and commitment pulled moderately toward the same concerning outcome, reflecting patterns where such profiles have historically faced steeper odds. It wasn't any one thing, but this combination painted a picture of a student the model sees as needing more boost to clear the bar's high bar, much like others who've stumbled despite their determination. This isn't the end of your story—you have the fire to rewrite it, perhaps by targeting schools with stronger support or building those grades higher. We're rooting for you to prove the odds wrong.
